_meta.note: quiz tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: The Inscription War — Dopamine Master Quiz
html_001: Tongcheon Domyeong · 2025 · A Grand Ancient-History Expedition
html_002: The ancient history Imperial Japan erased, reclaimed from the stele
html_005: can rewrite an entire people’s history.”
html_007: Learn the core claims, context, and keywords of this book — born from a single stele — through
html_009:
.
        Start easy, then climb steadily harder,
html_010: and every wrong question comes back until you nail it.
html_027: That’s the end of the free preview
html_040: The Inscription War at a glance
html_043: and you’ll never forget it. It all starts with
html_044: Imperial Japan’s history distortion (the Mimana theory)
html_047: 🗾 Imperial Japan’s hunger to rule the Korean Peninsula
html_048: Seikanron — they needed a pretext to justify invasion
html_051: Citing the Nihon Shoki, Empress Jingū year 49 (369 CE)
html_053: 🖌️ Tampering with the inscriptions (the Stele & the pagoda stele)
html_054: Changing ‘二’ to ‘海’ on the Stele, rigging the rubbings
html_057: ‘倭寇□潰’ and more — one character flips the meaning
html_059: 🏯 Restoring the truth of ancient history
html_060: “Even so, the truth could not be hidden forever”
html_062: Table of contents at a glance
html_064: 3 cases from the book worth pondering
html_066: Focused review of what you missed
html_068: lets you dig deeper into the original inscriptions and the debates.
html_074: A repeat master quiz that locks a book’s core in through dopamine
html_075: This app is secondary learning content that reworks the book’s key concepts for study. For the full reading experience, turn to the original book.
html_076: The Inscription War · Find the book

js_004: Who is Ven. Domyeong, the author of 『The Inscription War』?
js_005: A monk — abbot of Yeoyeo-jeongsa and deputy director of Beomeosa’s museum
js_006: A history professor teaching archaeology at a university
js_007: A national history committee member who spent years cataloguing colonial-era records
js_008: A Korean-Japanese journalist based in Japan
js_009: The author, Ven. Domyeong, is abbot of Yeoyeo-jeongsa in Miryang, South Gyeongsang, deputy director of the Beomeosa Museum of Buddhist Art, and chair of the Gaya Culture Promotion Foundation. He was ordained at Beomeosa in 1998 (under Ven. Jeongyeo), and from 2009 he preached in Gimhae, where he grew close to Gaya history.
js_011: Which two steles does this book dig into side by side?
js_012: The Gwanggaeto Stele and the Jingyeong Stele
js_013: The Gwanggaeto Stele and King Jinheung’s tour steles
js_014: The Jungwon Goguryeo Stele and the Sataekjijeok Stele
js_015: King Muryeong’s tomb tablet and the Seven-Branched Sword
js_016: As its subtitle says, the book tackles ‘the truth of the Gwanggaeto Stele and the Jingyeong Stele’. These two inscriptions are two of the three grounds Imperial Japan cited for a ‘Mimana in the Korean Peninsula,’ and they were long abused as tools of historical distortion.
js_018: Where does the Gwanggaeto Stele stand today?
js_019: Ji’an, Jilin, China (the old Manchurian Tonggou)
js_020: At the foot of Daeseongsan Fortress, Pyongyang
js_021: In a vault at the National Museum of Korea, Seoul
js_022: In the garden of the Tokyo National Museum, Japan
js_023: The Stele stands in Ji’an, Jilin (集安), China — the old Manchurian Tonggou region. It is one of the largest epigraphs recording Goguryeo history, yet its reading has been debated for nearly 150 years.
js_025: Which theory did Imperial Japan push to justify invading the peninsula?
js_030: It’s the Mimana (Imna Ilbonbu) theory — the claim that ancient Japan ruled ‘Imna (Mimana)’ in the southern Korean Peninsula. The author traces how, to justify it, Imperial Japan altered and distorted both inscriptions.
js_032: Which two passages on the Stele does the author say Imperial Japan altered?
js_035: The founding-myth and the tomb-keeper passages
js_037: The book’s central claim is that the Japanese Army General Staff altered the Sinmyo-year (辛卯年) passage and the Gyeongja-year (庚子年) passage. It back-traces the why, the how, and the whole process, one step at a time, like a detective chasing a suspect.
js_039: Which institution does the book name as the mastermind of the tampering?
js_041: The colonial Government-General’s Bureau of Education
js_044: It’s the Japanese Army General Staff. Noting Imperial Japan’s playbook — send spies ahead of an invasion to study and doctor the target nation’s history — the author traced the General Staff’s aims and methods in the tampering.
js_046: Which General Staff spy carried off a rubbing of the Stele from Manchuria in 1883?
js_047: Lt. Sakawa Kageaki, posing as a herbalist
js_048: Itō Hirobumi, disguised as a wandering monk
js_049: Saigō Takamori, disguised as a merchant
js_050: Fukuzawa Yukichi, disguised as a schoolteacher
js_051: Dispatched in 1880, Lt. Sakawa Kageaki (酒勾景信) ran intelligence work disguised as an herbal doctor, came upon the stele at Tonggou, Manchuria, in 1883, obtained a rubbing (the double-outline ink copy), and brought it to the General Staff in 1884.
js_053: What is the standard reading of the Sinmyo-year passage that Imperial Japan spread?
js_054: ‘In the Sinmyo year Wa crossed the sea, smashed Baekjan and Silla, and made them subjects’
js_055: ‘In the Sinmyo year Goguryeo subdued Wa and received its tribute’
js_056: ‘Baekje and Silla joined forces and drove Wa back across the sea’
js_057: ‘In the Sinmyo year Gaya sealed an alliance with the Wa people’
js_058: The standard reading is ‘Wa crossed the sea (渡海破), crushed Baekjan and Silla, and made them subjects’. It has been treated as ‘hard proof’ of Wa rule over the southern peninsula — the Mimana theory — but the author, citing broken context and internal contradictions, is convinced it was altered.
js_060: How do Imperial Japan’s reading and the author’s reading of ‘草拔聖枝 (Chobalseongji)’ differ?
js_061: Japan reads it as ‘pulled a holy branch from the grass’; the author reads it as the ‘name’ of a defected Imna royal
js_062: Japan reads it as a place name; the author reads it as an official title
js_063: Japan reads it as a temple’s name; the author reads it as an era name
js_064: Both read it as a personal name, differing only in pronunciation
js_065: Imperial Japan turned nouns into verbs and adjectives to read it as ‘pulled a holy branch from the grass’, but the author holds that Chobalseongji is a person’s name — an Imna royal who, harassed by neighboring soldiers, defected to Silla, and a founding ancestor of the New Kim clan.
js_067: What does the author set straight about King Heungmu (Kim Yu-sin) in the inscription?
js_068: Not the master’s ancestor, but the one who accepted ancestor Chobalseongji’s defection
js_069: He truly is the master’s direct-line ancestor
js_070: He does not appear in the inscription at all
js_071: He was the very last king of Imna (Mimana)
js_072: The standard view held that ‘Kim Yu-sin was Master Jingyeong’s ancestor,’ tying Kim Yu-sin to the Imna (Gaya) royal line. Re-parsing the inscription, the author overturns this: Kim Yu-sin appears only because he accepted Chobalseongji’s surrender (投) — he is not the master’s ancestor.
js_079: The author is a monk, not a historian. Remember: he breaks the assumption that ‘no credential means no standing.’
js_080: Easy to mix up — the two steles come from different eras (Goguryeo for the Stele, Unified Silla for the pagoda stele), yet a single thread, ‘Imna,’ ties them together.
js_081: King Gwanggaeto was a conquering ruler, just as his name (‘the broad expander’) says. The Stele itself records those feats.
js_082: Common myth — the Stele is not in Korea but on old Goguryeo land, in Ji’an County, Jilin, China. That’s why access and rubbings became such a big issue.
js_083: Don’t confuse it with ‘Seikanron.’ Seikanron is the call to conquer Korea; the Mimana theory is the distorted ancient-history doctrine built to back it up.
js_084: The tampering wasn’t about destroying the stele — it was about ‘touching up the characters.’ That’s why comparing rubbings is the key to uncovering the truth.
js_085: ‘Let’s eat, Grandma’ vs ‘Let’s eat Grandma’ — one pause flips the whole meaning. In hanja inscriptions, that principle is decisive.
js_086: Changing a single character, ‘二 → 海,’ knotted up the entire context. It’s a prime case of a tiny edit breeding a huge distortion.
js_087: Keep it straight: the one in Yongji Park, Changwon, is a replica — the original is at the National Museum of Korea.
js_088: The book’s goal isn’t to ‘attack Japan’ but to ‘strip away the distortion and restore the truth.’ A balanced stance is the point.
js_103: The stele of King Gwanggaeto, Goguryeo’s 19th and conquering ruler. It stands in Ji’an County, Jilin, China, and is a key source for Goguryeo history.
js_104: A theory Imperial Japan built as a lever for conquering the peninsula, claiming Gaya was the old Imna — Japanese territory.
js_106: Imperial Japan’s doctoring of characters on the Stele to prop up the theory — turning the original ‘二’ into ‘海.’
js_108: With hanja, where you break changes the meaning — like ‘Let’s eat, Grandma’ vs ‘Let’s eat Grandma.’
js_109: The passages the author offers as proof of tampering. Using rubbings, he pins down the original form and the traces of doctoring.
js_110: A replica in Yongji Park, Changwon; the original at the National Museum of Korea. The stage for yet another historical distortion.
js_111: The New Kim clan and Imna royals
js_112: The master’s ancestors were Imna royals, with King Heungmu (Kim Yu-sin) as founder. The reading of ‘草拔聖枝’ is the key.
js_114: Get to know the many angles of the debate too — including views that treat the Sinmyo-year passage and the tampering as separate issues.
js_116: From Gaya to Imna, and on to the Stele
js_117: The author isn’t a historian but a monk who preached in Gimhae (the śramaṇa Domyeong).
js_118: While studying the history of Gaya and Gaya Buddhism, he runs into the ‘Imna (任那)’ problem.
js_119: Following the Imna records, he ends up examining the Gwanggaeto Stele itself.
js_120: So begins a journey that lays out, point by point — with documents and inscriptions — ‘how Imperial Japan distorted history.’
js_122: Korea’s biggest ancient-history issue · the Sinmyo-year passage
js_123: The Stele resurfaces as an altered record of its own age.
js_124: It walks through the life of the conqueror King Gwanggaeto and the value of the Stele.
js_125: See how the Stele was used as the ‘first ground’ for the Mimana theory.
js_126: It digs into the contested Sinmyo-year passage and the tampering evidence ‘倭寇□潰.’
js_127: It compares the contradictions in context, the methods of tampering, and the various readings.
js_129: Restoring the inscription · the aim of the tampering
js_130: It restores the Sinmyo-year passage and analyzes ‘討倭殘國’ in the pivotal 〈Byeongsin-year passage〉.
js_131: It gathers the documentary records that back up the Sinmyo- and Gyeongja-year passages.
js_132: It locates the aim of the tampering in ‘Seikanron and Yokoi Tadanao.’
js_133: It sums up the truth the rubbings tell, a ‘Goguryeo that crossed the sea,’ and the balance of power among the states.
js_136: Imna, the hot topic of Korea–Japan ancient history — it weighs the Tsushima theory and the Kyushu theory.
js_137: It pins down Imna’s bearings from the location of Gyerim in the 〈Sujin year-65 passage〉.
js_138: It explains that Imna moved, in terms of an ‘early Imna’ and a ‘late Imna.’
js_139: It argues over subjects vs. subject-people, Jongbal Fortress and the Alla garrison, and the location of the Silla fortresses.
js_140: It moves on to Goguryeo’s southern campaign (南征), Gaya’s decline, and setting the inscription and chronology straight.
js_142: The reawakening dream of the Great King
js_143: It examines the Great King’s revival and Japan’s attitude toward it.
js_144: It sets the Stele as a marker (標石) of the Korean people’s pride.
js_145: Part 1 closes with a summary and a transcription (釋文) of the Gwanggaeto Stele.
js_148: It introduces the pagoda stele’s overview and content, the New Kim clan, the Imna royals, and King Heungmu (Kim Yu-sin).
js_149: It covers the master’s ancestor and Imna royal ‘草拔聖枝,’ and the surrender of that ancestor, Chobalseongji.
js_150: It traces Imna’s location from the Imna records in the 〈Treatise on Wa〉 of the Book of Song and the reading of ‘草拔.’
js_151: It shows why ‘草拔聖枝’ could not be allowed to be a personal name, and why the surrender was made to Silla.
js_154: Deng Xiaoping’s black-cat-white-cat maxim — ‘as long as it catches mice’ — speaks to who is qualified to probe history.
js_155: History isn’t the exclusive domain of historians — sincerity and relentless inquiry are the credentials.
js_156: The book closes on a conviction: even if you change a single character, the truth can’t be hidden forever.
js_158: Drop the comma from ‘Let’s eat, Grandma’ and you get ‘Let’s eat Grandma’ — the meaning flips entirely. It’s an analogy for why parsing is decisive in hanja inscriptions.
js_159: It drives home how one seemingly trivial reading can change all of history.
js_161: When Imperial Japan altered the original ‘二’ into ‘海,’ the parsing and the context knotted up completely. It’s a case of a single doctored character becoming a tool of distortion.
js_162: It shows how tampering with one character can lead to a colossal distortion of history.
js_164: On a wrong reading of the Jingyeong Stele, some scholars mistook Gaya for Imna and Kim Yu-sin for an Imna royal. It’s a case where inscription reading feeds straight into questions of identity.
js_165: It shows how the reading of a single line can shake even a people’s sense of its own roots.
js_166: If one character, one parse, can change history, how solid are the records we believe to be ‘fact’?
js_167: Every source passes through interpretation. We have to ask who parsed it that way, and why.
js_168: Does history belong to historians alone? Without a degree, what decides your standing to uncover the truth?
js_169: The author’s answer: ‘sincerity and relentless inquiry.’ Chew on what the black-cat-white-cat maxim really means.
js_170: Faced with an ‘agenda-first theory’ like the Mimana theory, what can we use to tell true from false?
js_171: Cross-evidence — context, grammar, rubbings — becomes the weapon that strips distortion away.
js_173: You’ve seen clean through to the truth of the inscriptions
js_175: You’ve nailed almost every key issue
js_177: You’ve got the bones — one more round!
js_179: The more you repeat, the higher your score

_app.title: The Inscription War Quiz
_app.desc: The Gwanggaeto and Jingyeong steles — reclaiming an ancient history that was erased.
